title: Udham Singh (field hockey)

infobox.value: |  | | --- | --- | |  | | | Men's [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") | | | | Representing  [India](/wiki/India "India") | | | | [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") | | | | Gold medal – first place | [1952 Helsinki](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") | [Team](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1952_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the 1952 Summer Olympics") | | Gold medal – first place | [1956 Melbourne](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") | [Team](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the 1956 Summer Olympics") | | Gold medal – first place | [1964 Tokyo](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") | [Team](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the 1964 Summer Olympics") | | Silver medal – second place | [1960 Rome](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") | [Team](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1960_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the 1960 Summer Olympics") | | [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") | | | | Silver medal – second place | [1958 Tokyo](/wiki/1958_Asian_Games "1958 Asian Games") | [Team](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1958_Asian_Games "Field hockey at the 1958 Asian Games") | | | |

(4 August 1928 – 23 March 2000) was an Indian hockey player from [Sansarpur](/wiki/Sansarpur "Sansarpur"), [Jalandhar](/wiki/Jalandhar "Jalandhar"), [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(India) "Punjab (India)"), [India](/wiki/India "India"). He played in [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). He shares the distinction of being one of only two Indian players to win four Olympic medals, the other being [Leslie Claudius](/wiki/Leslie_Claudius "Leslie Claudius"). He scored 14 goals from total of 14 Olympic matches
Pandya, Haresh (9 May 2000). ["Udham Singh"](https://www.theguardian.com/news/2000/may/09/guardianobituaries3). *The Guardian*. Retrieved 16 August 2022.
Haresh Pandya (9 May 2000). ["Udham Singh"](https://www.theguardian.com/news/2000/may/09/guardianobituaries3). *The Guardian*. Retrieved 1 August 2021.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indian field hockey player

Udham Singh

**Udham Singh Kular** (4 August 1928 – 23 March 2000) was an Indian hockey player from [Sansarpur](/wiki/Sansarpur "Sansarpur"), [Jalandhar](/wiki/Jalandhar "Jalandhar"), [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(India) "Punjab (India)"), [India](/wiki/India "India"). He played in [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics") [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). He shares the distinction of being one of only two Indian players to win four Olympic medals, the other being [Leslie Claudius](/wiki/Leslie_Claudius "Leslie Claudius"). He scored 14 goals from total of 14 Olympic matches

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Udham was born on 4 August 1928 at Sansarpur, a small village near the [Jalandhar Cantonment](/wiki/Jalandhar_Cantonment_(Assembly_constituency) "Jalandhar Cantonment (Assembly constituency)") of Punjab. He studied at Victor High School and DAV College, Jalandhar.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Although Udham had a short stature of 5 feet 6 inches and weighed only 58 kg, it never affected his game.

## Domestic hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Domestic hockey")]

In 1947 Udham was named the Captain of his College Hockey team and was recruited by the Punjab Police the same year, which had one of the best Hockey teams in the nation at the time. For a period of 18 years he played for Punjab Police, and led the team a couple of times during the tenure. Udham was named the Captain of the State Hockey team of Punjab in 1954.

## International hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: International hockey")]

Udham Singh would have made his Olympics debut in London in 1948, but due to a finger injury he missed the chance. He played in a Hockey Series against Afghanistan in 1949, contributing to India’s victory. Udham was a part of the Indian Hockey squad at Helsinki Olympics in 1952 where Captain K.D. Singh Babu led the Indian team to a Gold Medal win, and at the Melbourne Olympics in 1956 where Balbir Singh Senior successfully led the Indian team to save the Gold Medal. Udham also played in the Rome Olympics in 1960 and at the Tokyo Olympics in 1964, his last Olympic games. At Rome, [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") defeated India in the Finals, while the Indian team snatched its Gold Medal back from Pakistan in Tokyo defeating the opponents by 1-0.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Udham delivered his best performance at the Tokyo Asian Games in 1958 but still couldn’t get India the Gold as Pakistan beat India on the basis of better goal average.

Udham Singh is one of the two hockey players to win 3 golds and a silver in the Olympics, the other being [Leslie Claudius](/wiki/Leslie_Claudius "Leslie Claudius"). He was also awarded the [Arjuna Award](/wiki/Arjuna_Award "Arjuna Award") by the [Indian Government](/wiki/Indian_Government "Indian Government"). He was a Half back but had the adaptability to play from Left Inside, Right Inside, Center Forward and Center Half positions as well.

## Captaincy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Captaincy")]

He was named the Captain of the [Indian Hockey Federation](/wiki/Indian_Hockey_Federation "Indian Hockey Federation") team that went to a Warsaw tour in 1955 and to [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") in 1959. Udham also led the Indian squad to World Cup Hockey held at [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), France.

## Coaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Coaching")]

Udham Singh served as the Coach of the Indian Hockey team and successfully got the team a bronze Medal at the Mexico Olympics in 1968 and the silver medal at the Bangkok Asian Games in 1970. After his retirement from playing hockey, he turned to coaching young male teams.

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Udham_Singh_(field_hockey)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Awards")]

* [Arjuna Award](/wiki/Arjuna_Award "Arjuna Award") in 1965.
